antiPatterns: The 'MOBBIN.COM/SLACK' branding lockup uses all-caps on a URL string, which violates the typographic convention that URLs should preserve case-sensitivity for technical accuracy—even though Slack handles case-insensitively, the visual suggests the literal URL when it's actually a namespace label

voice.tone: Casual-declarative with parenthetical asides that acknowledge user awareness—'Nothing here (yet!)' presumes the user knows why the void exists rather than over-explaining the empty state
examples: Add SLMobbin to Slack to start saving screens
avoid: Apologetic empty-state language ('Sorry, no items found')
Over-enthusiastic onboarding ('Let's get started!' or 'Welcome aboard!')
Feature-marketing speak that front-loads benefits before acknowledging current state

entries.title: RUN

typePairing.notes: The hierarchy relies on a dramatic scale jump between the 12px all-caps utility labels and the 48px serif display headline, creating authoritative emphasis without intermediate steps. Body text at 14px maintains a single weight across all functional contexts, forcing location and color (#8c745c for secondary, #312c1c for primary) to differentiate importance rather than weight variation.

entries.critique: The icon-only left rail (24px monochrome glyphs at fixed vertical rhythm) DECISION creates instant pattern recognition through position memory rather than label scanning EFFECT, rejecting the tab-bar-with-labels pattern that demands cognitive reading overhead REJECTION. The full-bleed photographic card backgrounds DECISION eliminate the need for artificial container shapes, letting actual imagery define boundaries EFFECT, avoiding the default rounded-rectangle-grid that treats every content type as equivalent abstract data REJECTION. The accent color (#1a94e4) appears only in progress visualization and interactive controls DECISION, making every blue element inherently actionable EFFECT, rejecting the decorative-accent approach where brand color appears in static headings or backgrounds and dilutes interaction signaling REJECTION. The serif display type at 48px paired with 12px sans-caps labels DECISION uses style contrast (not just size) to mark editorial voice versus system chrome EFFECT, avoiding the single-typeface-family convention that forces weight and size alone to carry all hierarchical meaning REJECTION.
whatToSteal: Use monochrome icon navigation with exactly zero labels when vertical position becomes the mnemonic device — the Y-axis location is more memorable than reading a word, especially for <6 destinations

Restrict your accent color to interactive elements and progress indicators exclusively; if something is blue, it must respond to input or show dynamic state — this trains users that color means 'this will do something'

Pair all-caps 12px utility labels directly above (not beside) 48px+ serif headlines to create a two-tier voice system: the whisper that categorizes versus the voice that declares, using style contrast to make the size jump feel intentional rather than erratic
Let photographic content define card boundaries through full-bleed placement instead of drawing boxes around images — the subject matter becomes the container, eliminating redundant geometric framing

antiPatterns: The bottom navigation bar uses text-only labels without icons, creating ambiguity about their tap targets and failing to provide the dual-encoding (word + symbol) that accelerates recognition — especially problematic when the left rail demonstrates that icons alone suffice for navigation, making the inconsistency feel arbitrary rather than context-driven

voice.tone: Aspirational-imperative: the copy treats goals as assumed certainties ('Your Roadmap', not 'Build a roadmap') and uses possessive pronouns to imply the user already owns their transformation journey, framing the interface as a companion to pre-existing commitment rather than a persuasive tool trying to manufacture motivation.
avoid: Encouragement language ('Let's get started!', 'You can do it!')
Explanatory wayfinding ('Here's where you'll find...', 'Track your progress here')
Gamified celebration ('Awesome!', 'Level up!', 'You're crushing it!')

entries.title: SLMobbin

typePairing.notes: The interface relies on extreme scale contrast rather than weight variation: metric numerals appear roughly 3× the size of their labels with tight line spacing, forcing the eye to register magnitude before reading context. Labels use identical styling whether interactive ('24h'/'7d') or static, eliminating typographic hierarchy in favor of spatial grouping.

entries.critique: The 1:2.5 icon-to-gap ratio in the left rail (16px icons with 40px spacing) creates breathing room that prevents pattern-matching fatigue during quick scanning—rejecting the typical 1:1 or 1:1.5 ratios that force users to parse icon shapes rather than letting vertical rhythm guide movement. The data cards use zero padding between metric and label, stacking them into a single visual unit where the number's mass anchors the meaning—avoiding the conventional 8-12px gap that treats metric and label as separate components requiring conscious association. The graph line's exclusive use of #0484dc reserves chromatic attention for data signals, never diluting it with interactive element highlighting—rejecting the common pattern of repeating accent colors across buttons, links, and selections, which trains users to ignore color as semantically meaningful.
whatToSteal: Zero-padding metric-label stacks: place the contextual label immediately beneath the number with no vertical gap, letting typeface size difference (3× ratio minimum) create the hierarchy instead of whitespace—eliminates the association delay caused by spatial separation

Chromatic reservation for data: assign your accent color exclusively to visualization elements, never to UI chrome or controls—preserves the perceptual link between 'colored thing' and 'thing representing information', making graphs decode faster

Asymmetric icon-gap ratios: use vertical spacing 2-2.5× larger than icon dimensions in navigation rails—the exaggerated rhythm lets peripheral vision track position without requiring foveal focus on each glyph

Background-foreground luminance jumps: use 5:1+ contrast ratios between canvas and content cards to create containment through luminance rather than borders—the optical 'pop' makes cards feel layered without requiring stroke rendering or shadow blur

antiPatterns: Typographically identical filter states: the '24h' and '7d' options show no visual distinction between active and inactive states—users must rely on memory or trial-and-error clicking rather than scanning to determine current selection, increasing cognitive load on every return visit to the interface

entries.title: SLMobbin

typePairing.notes: The design establishes hierarchy through color weight rather than size variation—the form labels use muted gray that recedes while the input values would appear in darker text, creating a visual cadence without dramatic scale shifts. The tight vertical rhythm between label and field suggests a compressed information density that prioritizes compactness over breathing room.

entries.critique: The tan modal background (#9c8267) creates warmth against typical dashboard grays, producing a tactile quality that separates the overlay from workspace chrome—this rejects sterile white modals that blend into application backgrounds, making the form feel like a distinct object users handle rather than a system dialog. The borderless close 'X' with no hit-state container forces precision but eliminates visual noise, trading safety margins for spatial economy—conventional circled close buttons add 24-32px of visual weight this design reclaims. Full-width button geometry converts the entire bottom edge into activation area, establishing a definitive endpoint that pulls the eye downward through the form—this abandons centered or right-aligned CTAs that create ambiguous negative space users must interpret.
whatToSteal: Tan/warm neutral backgrounds for modal overlays that need to feel like physical cards lifted from the interface rather than system dialogs—works when the base application uses cool grays or whites

Tone-on-tone input borders (darker value of the same hue family) when you want field containers to exist without shouting—requires careful contrast management but removes the 'form grid' aesthetic of hard borders
Full-width primary actions in constrained vertical layouts to create a bottom anchor that resolves visual tension—eliminates the need for users to hunt for the commit button
Uncontained close icons (just the glyph, no background shape) in layouts where every pixel of chrome competes with content—only works if hit targets remain above 40px despite visual minimalism

antiPatterns: Light gray labels (#bcc4d4) on tan backgrounds fail WCAG contrast requirements, forcing users with any visual impairment to strain—this appears to prioritize aesthetic restraint over legibility, a choice that excludes users rather than serving them

voice.tone: Terse, system-oriented labels with no explanatory text or inline help
avoid: Conversational prompts ('What should we call you?')
Reassuring microcopy ('We'll never share this')
Action-oriented button labels ('Sign me in', 'Let's go')

entries.title: BU

typePairing.notes: The design enforces hierarchy through color differentiation rather than size or weight variation — navigation labels remain identical in scale while toggling between dark brown (#46311c) for active states and muted tan (#b18e59) for inactive states. This approach demands that users parse state through hue memory rather than the more conventional bold-versus-regular distinction.

entries.critique: The search field's cyan monochrome treatment (#8cf4ec for both border and placeholder text) creates a visual anchor that doubles as wayfinding — the DECISION is using the accent color as the sole chromatic element in the top bar, the EFFECT is that search becomes the primary suggested action without requiring larger size or button styling, and this REJECTS the standard gray placeholder convention which would hide the field until moused over. The navigation state signaling through #46311c versus #b18e59 — the DECISION is a 50-unit lightness shift in the brown family for active/inactive states, the EFFECT is maintaining color temperature consistency while differentiating selection, and this REJECTS the typical blue highlight or background fill which would introduce a new hue family and break the earth-tone palette. The white-bordered avatar containers in #88bdb8 — the DECISION is layering a stroke over a desaturated teal fill, the EFFECT is creating depth through edge definition rather than shadow or scale, and this REJECTS the flat circle or gradient-ring approaches that rely on dimension tricks rather than clean geometry.
whatToSteal: Accent-color search placeholder (#8cf4ec text matching border) — makes the input field discoverable without enlarging it or adding icons, useful when search isn't the primary action but needs visual priority over surrounding chrome

Hue-family state toggling (#46311c to #b18e59 for nav selection) — maintains palette cohesion while signaling interaction, applicable when your design already commits to a restricted color set and can't afford blue highlights

Stroke-defined avatar containers (white border on #88bdb8 fill) — creates containment through edge contrast instead of shadow depth, works when your background is already light and drop shadows would create visual noise

Icon-only left rail navigation with zero labels — commits fully to recognition over recall, only viable when your icon set is industry-standard (home, settings, users) and your user base skews expert rather than occasional

antiPatterns: The label-free icon navigation on the left relies entirely on symbol recognition without text fallbacks — this fails for any non-standard functions or users unfamiliar with the specific icon language, creating a memorization tax that compounds with each new feature added to the sidebar

Using color value alone (#b18e59 vs #46311c) to signal navigation state creates an accessibility failure for colorblind users and low-contrast displays — there's no underline, background fill, or weight change to provide redundant encoding of the selected state

voice.tone: Absent — no instructional copy, labels, or messaging visible beyond proper nouns and the single-word 'Search' placeholder
avoid: Helper text or empty-state messaging
Conversational prompts or onboarding language

entries.title: Aboard

typePairing.notes: Hierarchy emerges through italic emphasis on key nouns ('first Aboard') rather than weight changes, keeping the primary message conversational. The chat interface avoids typical heading structures entirely, relying on sender name labels in smaller size and reduced opacity to distinguish meta-information from message content.

entries.critique: The DECISION to place #456bee blue on the message bubble background rather than interactive elements (the 'Get started' link uses underlined black text) creates an EFFECT where brand color signals information source rather than clickability, training users to associate blue with system-authored content. This REJECTS the convention of reserving primary brand colors for CTAs, which would compete visually with the conversational flow and make every blue element feel like a demand for action. The DECISION to set icons at ~14:1 contrast (#302e2d on #fcecd4) in the rail navigation produces an EFFECT of immediate recognizability without requiring hover states or labels, making the six-icon set function as a persistent orientation layer. This REJECTS the 4.5:1 minimum WCAG standard as insufficient for glanceable navigation, acknowledging that icons already carry higher cognitive load than text. The DECISION to stroke the message input field while leaving card containers stroke-free creates an EFFECT of invitation, where the single bordered element on screen becomes the natural focus target without requiring placeholder text or explicit instruction. This REJECTS the approach of uniform border treatment across containers, which would flatten the functional hierarchy between passive content and active input zones.
whatToSteal: Reserve the primary brand color for content provenance rather than interaction—apply it to message bubbles, info cards, or system-generated content so users learn to associate the color with 'this came from us' rather than 'click this now', reducing false affordances in content-heavy interfaces

Push icon contrast to 14:1 or higher in persistent navigation rails—WCAG minimums assume text legibility, but icons require instant pattern recognition from peripheral vision, and the additional contrast eliminates the need for labels or hover states

Apply borders to exactly one container type—the input field or form area—while leaving all passive content borderless, so the stroke itself becomes the affordance that says 'you can type here' without placeholder text or icons

Position sender avatars 8-10px outside the message bubble's left edge rather than aligning them, creating a hanging indent that visually anchors the chat thread while keeping avatars grouped with their messages rather than floating in a separate column

antiPatterns: The message input field sits at the canvas bottom with no visible character count, send button, or formatting controls, creating ambiguity about whether this is a single-line quick reply or a full composition area—users accustomed to expanded composers may hesitate to start typing without seeing the full affordance set first

voice.tone: The copy uses second-person address with contracted verbs ('Welcome to your first Aboard') and frames features as collaborative tools ('a place to gather') rather than productivity gains, establishing intimacy through conversational rhythm and possessive framing ('your first') that positions the product as belonging to the user from moment zero.
examples: An Aboard is a place to gather
avoid: Imperative commands without subject ('Create your first board', 'Start organizing')
Efficiency or productivity framing ('Save time', 'Boost your workflow')
Third-person product description ('Aboard helps you', 'The platform allows')
